results.possibleIdentity: Warren E. Dion was a prominent electronics engineer and WWII veteran based in Bristol and Terryville, Connecticut. He graduated from the University of Connecticut in 1943, where he was one of the key students responsible for establishing and building the university's radio station, WHUS (then the Husky Network). Dion spent much of his professional career as the lead electrical engineer for the Arthur G. Russell Company in Bristol, a firm specializing in the design and manufacture of special-purpose automation machinery and machine controls. His technical expertise spanned circuit design, instrumentation, and industrial automation. An avid amateur radio operator with the call signs W1BBH and N1BBH, he contributed technical articles to amateur radio publications, most notably a June 1987 piece in QST magazine regarding the design of battery chargers for gel-cell batteries. Beyond his industrial engineering work, Dion was a skilled recorder player and a member of the American Recorder Society. He demonstrated an early interest in the intersection of art and technology by designing custom electronic components, such as specialized clip-on microphone systems, to enhance the acoustic recorder with modern high-tech electronics. This multidisciplinary work aligns with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He was the brother of Jeannette Dion Petke and Joan Dion Buch.
